LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE JAN-12-2022 HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT SETON PACIFIC CONSTRUCTION'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. CROSS-DEFENDANT SETON'S DEMURRERS ARE DEEMED MOOT OR ARE OVERRULED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS.) COUNSEL FOR 1532 HARRISON IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER, JR.; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR NO. 8600, EMAIL: MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-12-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT SETON PACIFIC CONSTRUCTION'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-defendant Seton's demurrer to the 1535 Harrison/Build, Inc. cross-complaint is moot as to Build, Inc. since that entity does not plead any claims against Seton.

The Court grants Seton's request for judicial notice of the complaint and attached subcontract since both parties refer to the subcontract. (See Ingram v. Flippo (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1280, 1285, fn.3 [taking judicial notice of letter that was referenced in complaint (but not attached) where both parties referred to the letter and quoted from it].) On demurrer, the court will generally defer to plaintiff/cross-complainant's construction of an agreement where the instrument is ambiguous and reasonably susceptible to plaintiff/cross-complainant's interpretation. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239.)

Seton's demurrers to cause of action one for breach of contract are overruled. The cross-complaint clearly alleges a written agreement and demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored. A reasonable reading of section 1.2 of the subcontract shows that Seton agreed to be bound by the terms of the general contract. "Under California law, parties may validly incorporate by reference into their contract the terms of another document." (Baker v. Aubry (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 1259, 1264; see also Boys Club of San Fernando Valley, Inc. v. Fidelity & Deposit Co. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1266, 1271.) 1532 Harrison therefore pleads a breach of contract claim.

Seton's demurrers to cause of action two for breach of third party beneficiary contract are overruled. Seton fails to demonstrate that 1532 Harrison is not a third party beneficiary of the subcontract as a matter of law. Section 6.10 of the subcontract provides that no "person" shall be considered a third party beneficiary of the agreement. 1532 Harrison is not a "person," but rather a LLC. The agreement is therefore ambiguous with regard to 1532 Harrison. In addition, several other cited provisions of the subcontract demonstrate an intent to benefit 1532 Harrison. (See, e.g., Secs. 2.13, 5.1, 6.5.)

Counsel for 1532 Harrison is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
